What 81,000 Claude Users Around the World Want from AI
summary: Anthropic conducted the largest multilingual qualitative study ever, interviewing 81,000 Claude users across 159 countries in 70 languages about their AI usage, hopes, and fears. The research reveals that most people experience hope and alarm as coexisting tensions rather than opposing viewpoints, with concrete examples ranging from improved diagnoses to job displacement concerns.
takeaway: Understanding your users' real, ground-level concerns about AI tools—not just abstract risks—is essential for product development and stakeholder communication.

title: Services: The New Software – Why the Next $1T Company Will Sell Outcomes, Not Tools
summary: Bek argues that as AI models improve, the competitive advantage shifts from selling AI tools to selling services powered by AI—where the work itself becomes the product. He distinguishes between intelligence work (which AI now handles autonomously) and judgment work (which requires human experience), suggesting that software engineering was first to cross this threshold and other professions will follow.
takeaway: FP&A leaders should consider whether their finance teams should shift from building internal AI tools to outsourcing intelligence-heavy work (like close procedures or forecasting) to AI-powered service providers, freeing internal staff for high-judgment activities like strategic planning.
author_role: Venture capital investor at Sequoia Capital

title: AI in Finance: The Order of Operations—Why Tools Aren't Enough
summary: Subha Kumar argues that finance teams struggle with AI not because of tool gaps, but because they lack a strategic order of operations. He outlines a five-step framework: warm up with education, eliminate waste before automating, identify quick wins, document with TRACE criteria (Traceable, Repeatable, Auditable, Compliant, Explainable), and limit tools to 2-3 to avoid 'AI brain fry'.
takeaway: Before automating anything, map your workflows, kill the garbage work, and build a TRACE-compliant implementation strategy that your CFO can understand in 60 seconds.
author_role: Chartered Accountant, AI in Finance thought leader

title: Claude Code Auto Mode: Balancing AI Autonomy with Safety Controls
summary: Anthropic introduced Claude Code auto mode to address approval fatigue while maintaining safety—a middle ground between manual permission prompts and unrestricted AI execution. The system uses model-based classifiers to automatically approve safe actions while catching dangerous ones misaligned with user intent.
takeaway: For teams using AI coding assistants, auto mode offers a way to reduce permission friction without sacrificing security by letting models evaluate whether actions are genuinely user-intended.

title: AI in Finance: A Practical Order of Operations for Finance Teams
summary: Subha Kumar outlines a five-step framework for implementing AI in finance teams, emphasizing that successful adoption requires strategic planning, not just tools. The approach prioritizes eliminating waste before automating, starting with quick wins, and ensuring any AI implementation is traceable, repeatable, auditable, compliant, and explainable.
takeaway: Before automating with AI, map all team processes, ruthlessly eliminate non-value work, then start with repetitive rule-based tasks while applying the TRACE framework to ensure finance can stand behind the results.
author_role: Finance leader at Cerebrix with expertise in AI implementation

title: Where AI Value and Profits Actually Accrue Today—And Why the Stack Is Inverted
summary: Agrawal analyzes the current economics of generative AI, showing that semiconductor makers (especially Nvidia) capture ~83% of revenues and ~90% of profits—the inverse of cloud computing's distribution. He explores why the AI stack is A-shaped rather than V-shaped and questions where value will shift as the market matures.
takeaway: As an FP&A leader, understand that today's AI economics are dominated by hardware suppliers, not software applications—a structural advantage that may not persist as models commoditize and inference costs fall.

title: Custom AI Chips Reaching Inflection Point: Can Broadcom and Others Challenge Nvidia's Dominance?
summary: Custom AI chips (ASICs/XPUs) from Broadcom, Marvell, and others are reaching an inflection point as major tech companies like OpenAI, Anthropic, and Meta seek cheaper, more efficient alternatives to Nvidia GPUs for their specific workloads. Broadcom's strategic advantage lies in bundling custom chips with its networking infrastructure, creating complementary products that serve large cloud providers and AI companies.
takeaway: FP&A teams tracking AI infrastructure spend should understand that custom chip adoption could reshape capex budgets and vendor concentration risk as major cloud providers shift away from Nvidia's premium-priced GPUs toward tailored, cost-optimized solutions.

title: Google Cloud's Ironwood TPU Hits General Availability with 10x Performance Gains
summary: Google Cloud announced general availability of Ironwood, its seventh-generation TPU designed for AI inference workloads, offering 10x peak performance over TPU v5p and 4x performance per chip for training versus TPU v6e. The announcement reflects the hyperscaler strategy to deploy custom chips that reduce Nvidia dependency and improve price-performance ratios for AI operations.
takeaway: Organizations evaluating AI infrastructure should monitor custom chip offerings from hyperscalers as viable alternatives to traditional GPUs, particularly for inference-heavy workloads where cost optimization matters most.

title: NVIDIA Launches Rubin Platform: Six-Chip AI Supercomputer with 10x Inference Cost Reduction
summary: NVIDIA announced the Rubin platform, comprising six new chips designed to reduce inference token costs by up to 10x and GPU requirements for MoE model training by 4x compared to Blackwell. The platform includes new CPUs, GPUs, networking switches, and storage processors optimized for large-scale AI systems, with early adoption from Microsoft and CoreWeave.
takeaway: FP&A teams should track infrastructure cost efficiency improvements like Rubin's 10x inference cost reduction, as lower compute costs directly impact AI project ROI and capital efficiency metrics.

title: The Danger of Hiding Bad Metrics: A Cautionary Cartoon on Data Integrity
summary: A humorous cartoon satirizes the flawed logic of eliminating complaint mechanisms to artificially improve satisfaction metrics. The cartoon highlights how removing the means to capture negative feedback doesn't solve underlying problems—it just creates blind spots.
takeaway: Don't fall into the trap of managing metrics instead of managing the business; eliminating feedback channels masks real issues rather than solving them.

title: Subscription Model Humor: A Take on Modern Parenting Economics
summary: A humorous cartoon that plays on the subscription economy by imagining parents offering their children a subscription model instead of a traditional allowance. The joke captures the trend of recurring revenue models permeating everyday life.
takeaway: A lighthearted reminder that subscription and recurring revenue models have become so prevalent they're now fodder for family humor.

title: The Illusion of Improvement: When Metrics Hide Problems
summary: A satirical cartoon highlighting a common business pitfall: improving metrics by eliminating the mechanisms that measure problems rather than solving them. The joke illustrates how removing complaint forms can artificially boost satisfaction scores while real issues remain unaddressed.
takeaway: Be skeptical of metric improvements that coincide with changes to measurement systems—ensure your KPIs reflect actual performance, not just the absence of bad news.

title: A Framework for Finding Fulfillment at Work and in Life
summary: Jason Cohen explores what creates fulfillment in work and life, drawing on frameworks like Daniel Pink's motivation theory (autonomy, mastery, purpose). He shares a personal framework he developed a decade ago when stepping down as CEO, relevant to managers facing retention challenges in the modern workplace.
takeaway: Understanding intrinsic motivators—autonomy, mastery, and purpose—is more effective for retaining top talent and fostering fulfillment than traditional compensation and incentive structures.
author_role: Founder and former CEO of software company

title: Reposition Your Product's Value Proposition to Justify 8x Higher Pricing
summary: Jason Cohen demonstrates how the same product can command vastly different prices depending on how you position its value to customers. By shifting focus from cost-savings to growth enablement, a marketing tool priced at $5k/month based on savings could instead be positioned and priced at $40k/month based on revenue impact.
takeaway: Price based on the customer's most critical business outcome (growth, revenue) rather than a secondary benefit (cost reduction), even if the underlying product functionality remains identical.

title: Instead of Allowance, How About a Subscription Model?
summary: A humorous cartoon that uses a subscription business model joke to comment on modern parenting and monetization trends. The one-liner plays on the prevalence of subscription services by imagining a parent proposing a recurring billing model for their child's allowance.
takeaway: A lighthearted reminder that subscription models have permeated every aspect of modern life, even those outside traditional business contexts.

title: The Absurdity of Organizational Restructuring
summary: A humorous cartoon satirizes poorly planned corporate restructuring by depicting a manager reassigning roles in nonsensical ways, ultimately putting a copier machine in charge. The piece mocks how restructuring decisions often lack logic or clear purpose.
takeaway: Use this as a reminder that restructuring initiatives need clear strategic rationale and role clarity, or risk becoming organizational chaos that demoralizes teams.

title: When Restructuring Goes Wrong: A Humorous Take on Org Chart Chaos
summary: A business cartoon satirizing the absurdity of poorly planned restructuring efforts, where leadership reshuffles roles in nonsensical ways. The humor highlights how reorganizations can lose sight of actual business logic and accountability.
takeaway: Even when meant as a joke, this cartoon captures a real risk: restructuring initiatives that lack clear strategic rationale can erode team credibility and organizational coherence.

title: Why Mission and Vision Statements Matter (Even If They're Often Done Wrong)
summary: Jason Cohen explores the confusion around mission, vision, and purpose statements, noting that leaders and pundits can't even agree on definitions—yet these statements can still drive meaningful organizational behavior when done authentically rather than as marketing theater.
takeaway: Stop treating mission/vision as marketing exercises for your "About Us" page; instead, use them as genuine decision-making filters that actually guide where your organization invests time and resources.
author_role: Founder/writer at A Smart Bear; startup advisor

title: Why Solving a Real Problem Isn't Enough: The Hidden Gaps in Startup Business Models
summary: Cohen explains why many startups fail despite identifying real problems and building working solutions—the missing piece is a viable business model that reaches enough customers. Using Fermi estimation, he breaks down the math required to move from problem validation to sustainable revenue.
takeaway: Validating a problem and building a solution solving it are necessary but insufficient; you must rigorously model whether you can affordably reach enough customers at sufficient conversion rates to build a viable business.

title: Amazon Q4 2025 Results: Record Infrastructure Spending Powers 13.6% Revenue Growth
summary: Amazon reported Q4 2025 revenue of $213.4B (+13.6% YoY) with AWS growing 23.6% and reaching a $142B annualized run rate, driven by sustained AI and cloud infrastructure demand. takeaway: AWS's accelerating growth (fastest in 13 quarters) and expanding infrastructure backlog signal sustained enterprise AI investment demand, relevant for benchmarking cloud spend forecasting and computing cost assumptions.

title: Using the Needs Stack to Win Against Competitors
summary: Cohen introduces the Needs Stack framework, which distinguishes between what customers actually want (higher-level needs) and the means to achieve it (lower-level needs). He uses Charlie's AWS setup for WordPress as an example to show how companies often miss competitive opportunities by focusing on the wrong level of customer need.
takeaway: Position your product at the customer's true need, not just the tactical means—competitors who solve the higher-level problem will beat those who optimize the lower one.
author_role: Founder/Strategic advisor at A Smart Bear

title: Microsoft's Maia AI Chips Won't Replace Nvidia and AMD, Nadella Clarifies Strategy
summary: Microsoft deployed its homegrown Maia 200 AI inference chip in data centers, outperforming competing processors from Amazon and Google. CEO Satya Nadella clarified that despite building its own chips, Microsoft will continue purchasing from Nvidia and AMD rather than pursuing full vertical integration.
takeaway: Cloud providers building custom silicon should view it as complementary to, not a replacement for, best-in-class vendor chips—a multi-vendor strategy maintains innovation pace and supply flexibility.

takeaway: FP&A leaders should monitor GPU/TPU infrastructure costs and availability as a critical driver of AI project feasibility and competitive advantage within their organizations.

title: Hyperscaler Capital Expenditure Quadruples Since GPT-4 Launch, Driven by AI Infrastructure Investments

takeaway: FP&A leaders must account for accelerating capex cycles among key technology vendors when forecasting IT spending and evaluating cloud services pricing trends.

title: Meta's AI Investment Strategy Gains Wall Street Approval
summary: Meta's leadership received investor backing for aggressive AI infrastructure investments. The article covers Wall Street's confidence in the company's capital allocation strategy toward artificial intelligence development.

title: Replace MVP with SLC: Simple, Lovable, and Complete
summary: Jason Cohen argues that the MVP (Minimum Viable Product) methodology prioritizes learning at the expense of customer experience, and proposes SLC (Simple, Lovable, Complete) as a better framework. SLC maintains the speed and efficiency benefits of MVPs while ensuring the product is actually useful and desirable to customers from day one.
takeaway: Replace your MVP mindset with SLC: build something simple enough to ship quickly, lovable enough that customers enjoy using it, and complete enough that it solves a real problem without feeling unfinished.
author_role: Founder of A Smart Bear and WP Engine

title: Hyperscaler AI Infrastructure Capex Surges Past $600B in 2026, Forcing Tech Giants to Debt Markets
summary: The Big Five hyperscalers (Amazon, Google, Microsoft, Meta, Oracle) are projected to spend over $600B on capital expenditures in 2026, a 36% increase driven primarily by AI infrastructure investments. Global cloud infrastructure spending reached $102.6B in Q3 2025 with 25% year-over-year growth, signaling a shift from experimental AI to production-scale deployment.
takeaway: FP&A leaders planning infrastructure strategies should model for sustained capex intensity at hyperscalers and anticipate that debt financing will increasingly bridge the gap between AI investment needs and traditional cash generation—reshaping capital structures across the sector.
